items.q: In Pythagoras' theorem, which side does c^2 represent?
items.correct: The hypotenuse — the longest side, opposite the right angle.
items.misconception: c^2 represents any side of the triangle as long as the other two are squared.
items.random_wrong: c^2 represents the side opposite the largest angle that is not 90 degrees.
items.why: Students treat the formula as symmetric in a, b, c, missing that c specifically denotes the hypotenuse.

items.q: What is the formula for the area of a circle?
items.correct: pi * r^2 (pi times the radius squared).
items.misconception: The area of a circle is 2 * pi * r — the same as the circumference formula.
items.random_wrong: The area of a circle is pi * d, where d is the diameter.
items.why: Students remember 'pi and r' for circles but mix up the area formula with the circumference, since both involve pi and r.

items.q: What is the difference between area and perimeter?
items.correct: Perimeter is the total distance around the outside; area is the amount of surface inside (measured in square units).
items.misconception: Area and perimeter are different names for the same measurement.
items.random_wrong: Area is half the perimeter for any shape.
items.why: Students confuse the two terms because both describe the size of a shape, but they don't see that one is one-dimensional (length) and the other two-dimensional (length squared).
It's opposite ÷ hypotenuse. “adjacent ÷ hypotenuse” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Right triangle with legs 6 and 8. Hypotenuse?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
